name: San Rafael River
short_description: Public BLM land along the scenic San Rafael River offering free rockhounding for agate, jasper, and chalcedony specimens. Beautiful canyon setting with easy to moderate collecting.
description: The San Rafael River corridor in Emery County offers excellent rockhounding opportunities on public BLM land without permits or fees. This scenic area cuts through the heart of Utah's red rock country, exposing colorful agate nodules, jasper specimens, and chalcedony formations in wash gravels and hillside outcrops. The river has carved through multiple geological layers over millions of years, creating ideal conditions for finding quality specimens. Rockhounders can access the river valley via established roads and search the dry washes and benches where seasonal flooding has concentrated lighter minerals. The area is popular with rockhounders of all skill levels due to its accessibility and consistent gem quality.
history: The San Rafael River flows through the Basin and Range transitional geology where Precambrian metamorphic rocks and Paleozoic sediments have been fractured and mineralized by ancient hydrothermal activity. Agate and chalcedony deposits here formed during the Tertiary period when silica-rich fluids filled cavities and fractures in the surrounding host rock, creating the colorful nodules and banded specimens found today.
tips: Search the dry washes and stream gravels on both sides of the river where heavier agates and jaspers concentrate after seasonal flows. Look for the distinctive banded patterns and glassy luster of high-quality chalcedony on exposed hillsides and in loose talus. Bring a UV light to check specimens for fluorescence, which can help identify the best pieces before collecting. Early morning and late afternoon light enhances visibility of specimens among the red rock.
terrain_notes: Rolling red rock hills with elevation around 5,200 feet. Mixed pinyon-juniper vegetation with open areas of slickrock and sandy wash. Terrain varies from relatively flat river bottomland to steep hillsides. Walking distances from parking typically 0.5 to 2 miles depending on chosen collecting area. Footing is uneven with loose rock and slickrock sections requiring sturdy boots.
best_season: April - October. Spring (April-May) offers cooler temperatures and occasional runoff that exposes fresh material in washes. Fall (September-October) provides excellent weather with lower heat. Summer (June-August) is possible but afternoon temperatures can exceed 95 degrees F.
written_directions: From Castle Dale, take UT-29 east toward the San Rafael area for approximately 12 miles. Look for BLM access points and pullouts along the river corridor. The main access near the coordinates is via county roads on the north side of the river. A high-clearance vehicle is recommended, though passenger cars can reach several pullout areas during dry conditions. GPS navigation to 38.901794, -110.374884 will direct you to prime collecting terrain near the river bottom.
road_conditions: Rough dirt and gravel roads with washboard surfaces. High-clearance vehicle recommended. Some areas may be impassable after heavy rain.
parking_notes: Multiple informal pullouts and parking areas along access roads. No developed parking lot. Capacity limited to a few vehicles per pullout.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA regulations. No commercial collection. No motorized excavation or powered digging equipment. Collect only loose material - do not break rock formations or use explosives. Pack out all trash.
rules:
Personal use only
Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA

Do not break rock formations

Respect any posted closure areas
nearest_services: Castle Dale, approximately 15 miles west. Gas, food, and water available. Emery is approximately 20 miles south with additional services.
accessibility_notes: Relatively easy terrain for rockhounding with uneven footing and loose rock. Most collecting areas accessible without extensive hiking. Wheelchair access limited due to rough parking areas and slickrock terrain. Sturdy hiking boots strongly recommended. Not suitable for those with limited mobility due to uneven surfaces and rock scrambling in some areas.

faq.question: What gemstones can I find rockhounding at San Rafael River in Utah?
faq.answer: The San Rafael River is known for quality agate, jasper, chalcedony, and chert specimens. Most collectors find banded and multi-colored agate nodules in the river gravels and hillside outcrops. Jasper with red, brown, and tan coloring is also common. Specimens range from thumbnail size to several pounds.

faq.question: Do I need a permit to rockhound at San Rafael River?
faq.answer: No permit is required. San Rafael River sits on public BLM land where recreational rockhounding is allowed without permits or fees. You must follow standard BLM rules: personal use only, 25 lbs maximum per day, and no commercial collection.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to rockhound near Castle Dale?
faq.answer: April through October is ideal, with spring (April-May) and fall (September-October) offering the most comfortable temperatures. Summer heat can exceed 95 degrees F. Winter weather can make roads impassable. Seasonal runoff in spring exposes fresh material in washes.

faq.question: How far is San Rafael River from the nearest town with services?
faq.answer: Castle Dale is approximately 15 miles west of the San Rafael River collecting areas. The town offers gas stations, restaurants, and accommodations. Emery is about 20 miles south with additional services. Stock up on water before heading to remote collecting areas.

faq.question: Is rockhounding at San Rafael River good for beginners and families?
faq.answer: Yes, San Rafael River is beginner and family-friendly. The terrain is accessible, specimens are easy to spot in washes and on hillsides, and there are no permit hassles. Children ages 6 and up can participate. The 25 lb daily limit means you won't over-collect, and finding quality agates and jasper is almost guaranteed.

faq.question: What tools and equipment do I need for rockhounding the San Rafael area?
faq.answer: Bring a sturdy backpack, water (at least 2 liters), a small hammer or pick for extracting specimens from talus, a magnifying glass or UV light to inspect pieces, and a collecting bag. Wear sturdy hiking boots with ankle support due to uneven terrain and sharp rock. A GPS device or phone with maps helps navigation on multiple BLM access roads.

meta_description: Free BLM rockhounding for agate, jasper, and chalcedony at San Rafael River near Castle Dale. No permits required. Personal use collecting.
